sections.title: Client order recording for market abuse detection
sections.summary: Investment firms must maintain records proving client order terms and transaction correspondence to detect market abuse behavior. These records must cover all conversations with clients when dealing on own account, including alternative communication channels like emails and faxes. The records must be available to authorities for supervision and enforcement actions under relevant directives, and must demonstrate the development of client relationships. Firms must ensure no privately owned equipment is used for transactions to maintain legal certainty. Trading venues and investment firms must implement robust measures to prevent algorithmic trading from causing market disorder or abusive practices. Trading venues must ensure their systems are resilient to handle increased order flows and market stresses, including circuit breakers to halt trading during sudden price movements. Fee structures must be transparent, non-discriminatory, and fair to avoid promoting disorderly market conditions.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires detailed client order records to detect market abuse across borders, critical for DTIAs involving cross-border financial transactions and client data transfers. This section requires trading venues to implement technical safeguards for market stability during high-frequency trading, relevant to DTIA for cross-border trading systems.

Member States must allow venues to impose higher fees for frequent cancellations or high-frequency algorithmic trading to reflect system capacity burdens. Investment firms providing direct electronic market access must implement robust client suitability assessments and risk controls, retaining responsibility for client trades.

sections.title: Definitions for investment firm and related terms in financial services
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used throughout the Directive, including 'investment firm', 'investment services and activities', 'client', 'professional client', 'retail client', and other financial terms. sections.title: Definitions for investment services and financial entities
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used throughout the GDPR, including subsidiaries, groups, close links, management bodies, senior management, and specific financial terms like matched principal trading and algorithmic trading. These definitions establish the scope and meaning of critical concepts for investment firms and regulated markets operating within the EU.
sections.dtia_summary: These definitions clarify the scope of entities and activities subject to the directive, which is crucial for determining which data transfers and processing activities fall under the scope of the GDPR.

requirements.text: ‘subsidiary' means a subsidiary undertaking within the meaning of Articles 2(10) and 22 of Directive 2013/34/EU, including any subsidiary of a subsidiary undertaking of an ultimate parent undertaking
‘group' means a group as defined in Article 2(11) of Directive 2013/34/EU

‘close links' means a situation in which two or more natural or legal persons are linked by participation in the form of ownership, direct or by way of control, of 20 % or more of the voting rights or capital of an undertaking

‘control' means the relationship between a parent undertaking and a subsidiary, in all the cases referred to in Article 22(1) and (2) of Directive 2013/34/EU, or a similar relationship between any natural or legal person and an undertaking

‘management body' means the body or bodies of an investment firm, market operator or data reporting services provider, which are appointed in accordance with national law, which are empowered to set the entity's strategy, objectives and overall direction

‘senior management' means natural persons who exercise executive functions within an investment firm, a market operator or a data reporting services provider and who are responsible, and accountable to the management body, for the day-to-day management of the entity

‘matched principal trading' means a transaction where the facilitator interposes itself between the buyer and the seller to the transaction in such a way that it is never exposed to market risk throughout the execution of the transaction

‘algorithmic trading' means trading in financial instruments where a computer algorithm automatically determines individual parameters of orders such as whether to initiate the order, the timing, price or quantity of the order or how to manage the order after its submission, with limited or no human intervention
‘high-frequency algorithmic trading technique' means an algorithmic trading technique characterised by infrastructure intended to minimise network and other types of latencies

‘direct electronic access' means an arrangement where a member or participant or client of a trading venue permits a person to use its trading code so the person can electronically transmit orders relating to a financial instrument directly to the trading venue
‘cross-selling practice' means the offering of an investment service together with another service or product as part of a package or as a condition for the same agreement or package

‘structured deposit' means a deposit as defined in point (c) of Article 2(1) of Directive 2014/49/EU of the European Parliament and of the Council (1), which is fully repayable at maturity on terms under which interest or a premium will be paid or is at risk
‘transferable securities' means those classes of securities which are negotiable on the capital market, with the exception of instruments of payment

‘depositary receipts' means those securities which are negotiable on the capital market and which represent ownership of the securities of a non-domiciled issuer while being able to be admitted to trading on a regulated market

‘exchange-traded fund' means a fund of which at least one unit or share class is traded throughout the day on at least one trading venue and with at least one market maker which takes action to ensure that the price of its units or shares on the trading venue does not vary significantly from its net asset value
‘certificates' means certificates as defined in Article 2(1)(27) of Regulation (EU) No 600/2014
‘structured finance products' means structured finance products as defined in Article 2(1)(28) of Regulation (EU) No 600/2014
‘derivatives' means derivatives as defined in Article 2(1)(29) of Regulation (EU) No 600/2014
‘commodity derivatives' means commodity derivatives as defined in Article 2(1)(30) of Regulation (EU) No 600/2014
‘CCP' means a CCP as defined in Article 2(1) of Regulation (EU) No 648/2012

‘approved publication arrangement' or ‘APA' means a person authorised under this Directive to provide the service of publishing trade reports on behalf of investment firms pursuant to Articles 20 and 21 of Regulation (EU) No 600/2014

‘consolidated tape provider' or ‘CTP' means a person authorised under this Directive to provide the service of collecting trade reports for financial instruments listed in Articles 6, 7, 10, 12 and 13, 20 and 21 of Regulation (EU) No 600/2014 from regulated markets, MTFs, OTFs and APAs and consolidating them into a continuous electronic live data stream

‘approved reporting mechanism' or ‘ARM' means a person authorised under this Directive to provide the service of reporting details of transactions to competent authorities or to ESMA on behalf of investment firms

‘home Member State' means: in the case of investment firms, the Member State in which its head office is situated (if natural person), registered office (if legal person), or head office (if no registered office)
‘host Member State' means the Member State, other than the home Member State, in which an investment firm has a branch or provides investment services and/or activities

‘third-country firm' means a firm that would be a credit institution providing investment services or performing investment activities or an investment firm if its head office or registered office were located within the Union
‘wholesale energy product' means wholesale energy products as defined in point (4) of Article 2 of Regulation (EU) No 1227/2011

‘agricultural commodity derivatives' means derivative contracts relating to products listed in Article 1 of, and Annex I, Parts I to XX and XXIV/1, to, Regulation (EU) No 1308/2013 of the European Parliament and of the Council

‘sovereign issuer' means any of the following that issues debt instruments: the Union, a Member State, a federal Member State, a special purpose vehicle for several Member States, an international financial institution established by two or more Member States, or the European Investment Bank
‘sovereign debt' means a debt instrument issued by a sovereign issuer

‘durable medium' means any instrument which enables a client to store information addressed personally to that client in a way accessible for future reference and for a period of time adequate for the purposes of the information
‘data reporting services provider' means an APA, a CTP or an ARM

sections.title: Definitions for investment firm and related terms in financial services
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used throughout the Directive, including 'investment firm', 'investment services and activities', 'client', 'professional client', 'retail client', and other financial terms. It specifies that Member States may include non-legal persons as investment firms if they meet certain protection and supervision criteria. The definitions also cover specific financial instruments, trading venues, and market structures relevant to investment activities. This section lists specific financial regulations that are defined for the purpose of the GDPR. It identifies three directives related to UCITS, market transparency, and financial statements that are relevant to the context of the GDPR. These definitions are provided to clarify the scope of certain financial instruments and entities under the GDPR framework.

sections.summary: ESMA must issue guidelines by January 2016 on specific management body competency concepts including time commitment, knowledge, skills, integrity, training resources, and diversity considerations for market operators.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides detailed criteria for assessing management body competence, critical for DTIA compliance in market operator governance.

requirements.text: ESMA shall issue guidelines on sufficient time commitment of management body members
ESMA shall issue guidelines on adequate collective knowledge, skills and experience of management body
ESMA shall issue guidelines on honesty, integrity and independence of mind of management body members
ESMA shall issue guidelines on adequate human and financial resources for management body induction and training
ESMA shall issue guidelines on diversity considerations for management body selection

sections.source_quote: ESMA shall issue guidelines on the following: (a) the notion of sufficient time commitment of a member of the management body to perform that member's functions, in relation to the individual circumstances and the nature, scale and complexity of activities of the market operator; (b) the notion of adequate collective knowledge, skills and experience of the management body as referred to in point (b) of paragraph 2; (c) the notions of honesty, integrity and independence of mind of a member of the management body as referred to in point (c) of paragraph 2; (d) the notion of adequate human and financial resources devoted to the induction and training of members of the management body as referred to in paragraph 3; (e) the notion of diversity to be taken into account for the selection of members of the management body as referred to in paragraph 5.

sections.title: Position management controls reporting to competent authority
sections.summary: Investment firms or trading venue operators must inform their competent authority of position management controls. Competent authorities must share position limit details with ESMA, which publishes a database of position limits and controls for public access. This ensures transparency and oversight of position management across jurisdictions.

sections.title: Commission report on energy derivatives clearing impact
sections.summary: The Commission must prepare a report by 1 January 2018 after consulting ESMA and ACER to assess the potential impact on energy prices and market functioning, feasibility, and benefits of subjecting C6 energy derivative contracts to clearing obligations, risk mitigation techniques, and clearing thresholds. If the Commission determines it is not feasible or beneficial, it may submit a legislative proposal to the European Parliament and Council. The Commission may extend the 42-month period for delegated acts by two years once and one year further.

sections.title: Regular reporting of market participants to competent authorities
sections.summary: Member States must mandate market operators to periodically disclose the list of market participants to their relevant competent authorities. sections.title: Definitions for investment firm and related terms in financial services
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used throughout the Directive, including 'investment firm', 'investment services and activities', 'client', 'professional client', 'retail client', and other financial terms. It specifies that Member States may include non-legal persons as investment firms if they meet certain protection and supervision criteria. The definitions also cover specific financial instruments, trading venues, and market structures relevant to investment activities. This section lists specific financial regulations that are defined for the purpose of the GDPR. It identifies three directives related to UCITS, market transparency, and financial statements that are relevant to the context of the GDPR. These definitions are provided to clarify the scope of certain financial instruments and entities under the GDPR framework.

requirements.text: Member States may include in the definition of investment firms undertakings which are not legal persons, provided that: (a) their legal status ensures a level of protection for third parties' interests equivalent to that afforded by legal persons; and (b) they are subject to equivalent prudential supervision appropriate to their legal form.
Definitions of financial regulations relevant to the GDPR

sections.source_quote: For the purposes of this Directive, the following definitions apply: (1) ‘investment firm' means any legal person whose regular occupation or business is the provision of one or more investment services to third parties and/or the performance of one or more investment activities on a professional basis.

sections.title: ESMA develops standards for market data consistency
sections.summary: ESMA must develop draft regulatory technical standards specifying how CTPs comply with information obligations, the content of published information, financial instruments data requirements, and systems for ensuring data consistency across sources. And is capable of being aggregated at Union level
The concrete organisational requirements laid down in paragraphs 4 and 5

sections.source_quote: ESMA shall develop draft regulatory technical standards specifying: (a) the means by which the CTP may comply with the information obligation referred to in paragraphs 1 and 2; (b) the content of the information published under paragraphs 1 and 2; (c) the financial instruments data of which must be provided in the data stream and for non-equity instruments the trading venues and APAs which need to be included; (d) other means to ensure that the data published by different CTPs is consistent and allows for comprehensive mapping and cross-referencing against similar data from other sources, and is capable of being aggregated at Union level; (e) the concrete organisational requirements laid down in paragraphs 4 and 5.

sections.title: Definitions for investment firm and related terms in financial services
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used throughout the Directive, including 'investment firm', 'investment services and activities', 'client', 'professional client', 'retail client', and other financial terms. It specifies that Member States may include non-legal persons as investment firms if they meet certain protection and supervision criteria. sections.source_quote: ESMA shall develop draft regulatory technical standards to determine: (a) the criteria and methods for determining whether a position qualifies as reducing risks directly relating to commercial activities; (b) the methods to determine when positions of a person are to be aggregated within a group; (c) the criteria for determining whether a contract is an economically equivalent OTC contract to that traded on a trading venue, referred to in paragraph 1, in a way that facilitates the reporting of positions taken in equivalent OTC contracts to the relevant competent authority as determined in Article 58(2); (d) the definition of what constitutes the same commodity derivative and significant volumes under paragraph 6 of this Article; (e) the methodology for aggregating and netting OTC and on-venue commodity derivatives positions to establish the net position for purposes of assessing compliance with the limits. sections.title: Competent authorities' position limits and exceptions
sections.summary: Competent authorities cannot impose stricter position limits than those set by ESMA unless objectively justified and proportionate. They must publish any stricter limits for up to six months, renewable for six-month periods if justified, and notify ESMA of such decisions. ESMA must issue an opinion within 24 hours on the necessity of stricter limits.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows competent authorities to impose stricter limits with conditions, ensuring regulatory alignment while maintaining market liquidity and orderly functioning.

requirements.text: Competent authorities shall not impose limits more restrictive than those adopted by ESMA
Competent authorities may impose more restrictive limits in exceptional cases

Competent authorities must notify ESMA of more restrictive limits
ESMA must issue opinion within 24 hours on necessity of more restrictive limits
Competent authorities must publish notice explaining reasons for imposing limits contrary to ESMA opinion

sections.source_quote: Competent authorities shall not impose limits which are more restrictive than those adopted pursuant to paragraph 1 except in exceptional cases where they are objectively justified and proportionate taking into account the liquidity of the specific market and the orderly functioning of that market. Competent authorities shall publish on their website the details of the more restrictive position limits they decide to impose, which shall be valid for an initial period not exceeding six months from the date of their publication on the website. The more restrictive position limits may be renewed for further periods not exceeding six months at a time if the grounds for the restriction continue to be applicable. If not renewed after that six-month period, they shall automatically expire. Where competent authorities decide to impose more restrictive position limits, they shall notify ESMA. The notification shall include a justification for the more restrictive position limits. ESMA shall, within 24 hours, issue an opinion on whether it considers that the more restrictive position limits are necessary to address the exceptional case. The opinion shall be published on ESMA's website. Where a competent authority imposes limits contrary to an ESMA opinion, it shall immediately publish on its website a notice fully explaining its reasons for doing so.

sections.title: Definitions for investment firm and related terms in financial services
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used throughout the Directive, including 'investment firm', 'investment services and activities', 'client', 'professional client', 'retail client', and other financial terms. It specifies that Member States may include non-legal persons as investment firms if they meet certain protection and supervision criteria. The definitions also cover specific financial instruments, trading venues, and market structures relevant to investment activities. This section lists specific financial regulations that are defined for the purpose of the GDPR. It identifies three directives related to UCITS, market transparency, and financial statements that are relevant to the context of the GDPR. These definitions are provided to clarify the scope of certain financial instruments and entities under the GDPR framework.

requirements.text: Member States may include in the definition of investment firms undertakings which are not legal persons, provided that: (a) their legal status ensures a level of protection for third parties' interests equivalent to that afforded by legal persons; and (b) they are subject to equivalent prudential supervision appropriate to their legal form.
Definitions of financial regulations relevant to the GDPR

sections.source_quote: For the purposes of this Directive, the following definitions apply: (1) ‘investment firm' means any legal person whose regular occupation or business is the provision of one or more investment services to third parties and/or the performance of one or more investment activities on a professional basis.

sections.summary: ESMA must develop draft regulatory technical standards to define criteria for determining risk-reducing positions, methods for aggregating positions within groups, economically equivalent OTC contracts, commodity derivative definitions, netting methodologies, and exemption procedures. These standards must be submitted to the Commission by 3 July 2015 and may be delegated to the Commission for adoption.
sections.dtia_summary: ESMA's technical standards define precise methodologies for position aggregation and netting, critical for ensuring consistent cross-border data reporting and risk management in commodity derivatives trading.

requirements.text: ESMA must develop draft regulatory technical standards for determining risk-reducing positions
ESMA must develop draft regulatory technical standards for aggregating positions within groups
ESMA must develop draft regulatory technical standards for economically equivalent OTC contracts

ESMA must develop draft regulatory technical standards for netting methodologies
ESMA must develop draft regulatory technical standards for exemption procedures

sections.source_quote: ESMA shall develop draft regulatory technical standards to determine: (a) the criteria and methods for determining whether a position qualifies as reducing risks directly relating to commercial activities; (b) the methods to determine when positions of a person are to be aggregated within a group; (c) the criteria for determining whether a contract is an economically equivalent OTC contract to that traded on a trading venue, referred to in paragraph 1, in a way that facilitates the reporting of positions taken in equivalent OTC contracts to the relevant competent authority as determined in Article 58(2); (d) the definition of what constitutes the same commodity derivative and significant volumes under paragraph 6 of this Article; (e) the methodology for aggregating and netting OTC and on-venue commodity derivatives positions to establish the net position for purposes of assessing compliance with the limits. Such methodologies shall establish criteria to determine which positions may be netted against one another and shall not facilitate the build-up of positions in a manner inconsistent with the objectives set out in paragraph 1 of this Article; (f) the procedure setting out how persons may apply for the exemption under the second subparagraph of paragraph 1 of this Article and how the relevant competent authority will approve such applications; (g) the method for calculation to determine the venue where the largest volume of trading in a commodity derivative takes place and significant volumes under paragraph 6 of this Article.

sections.title: ESMA draft regulatory technical standards for position management
sections.summary: ESMA must develop draft regulatory technical standards to define criteria for determining risk-reducing positions, methods for aggregating positions within groups, economically equivalent OTC contracts, commodity derivative definitions, netting methodologies, and exemption procedures. These standards must be submitted to the Commission by 3 July 2015 and may be delegated to the Commission for adoption.

sections.source_quote: ESMA shall develop draft regulatory technical standards to determine: (a) the criteria and methods for determining whether a position qualifies as reducing risks directly relating to commercial activities; (b) the methods to determine when positions of a person are to be aggregated within a group; (c) the criteria for determining whether a contract is an economically equivalent OTC contract to that traded on a trading venue, referred to in paragraph 1, in a way that facilitates the reporting of positions taken in equivalent OTC contracts to the relevant competent authority as determined in Article 58(2); (d) the definition of what constitutes the same commodity derivative and significant volumes under paragraph 6 of this Article; (e) the methodology for aggregating and netting OTC and on-venue commodity derivatives positions to establish the net position for purposes of assessing compliance with the limits. Such methodologies shall establish criteria to determine which positions may be netted against one another and shall not facilitate the build-up of positions in a manner inconsistent with the objectives set out in paragraph 1 of this Article; (f) the procedure setting out how persons may apply for the exemption under the second subparagraph of paragraph 1 of this Article and how the relevant competent authority will approve such applications; (g) the method for calculation to determine the venue where the largest volume of trading in a commodity derivative takes place and significant volumes under paragraph 6 of this Article.

sections.heading: Competent authorities shall not impose limits which are more restrictive than those adopted pursuant to
sections.title: Competent authorities' position limits and exceptions
sections.summary: Competent authorities cannot impose stricter position limits than those set by ESMA unless objectively justified and proportionate. They must publish any stricter limits for up to six months, renewable for six-month periods if justified, and notify ESMA of such decisions. ESMA must issue an opinion within 24 hours on the necessity of stricter limits.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows competent authorities to impose stricter limits with conditions, ensuring regulatory alignment while maintaining market liquidity and orderly functioning.

requirements.text: Competent authorities shall not impose limits more restrictive than those adopted by ESMA
Competent authorities may impose more restrictive limits in exceptional cases

ESMA must issue opinion within 24 hours on necessity of more restrictive limits
Competent authorities must publish notice explaining reasons for imposing limits contrary to ESMA opinion

sections.source_quote: Competent authorities shall not impose limits which are more restrictive than those adopted pursuant to paragraph 1 except in exceptional cases where they are objectively justified and proportionate taking into account the liquidity of the specific market and the orderly functioning of that market. Competent authorities shall publish on their website the details of the more restrictive position limits they decide to impose, which shall be valid for an initial period not exceeding six months from the date of their publication on the website. The more restrictive position limits may be renewed for further periods not exceeding six months at a time if the grounds for the restriction continue to be applicable. If not renewed after that six-month period, they shall automatically expire. Where competent authorities decide to impose more restrictive position limits, they shall notify ESMA. The notification shall include a justification for the more restrictive position limits. ESMA shall, within 24 hours, issue an opinion on whether it considers that the more restrictive position limits are necessary to address the exceptional case. The opinion shall be published on ESMA's website. Where a competent authority imposes limits contrary to an ESMA opinion, it shall immediately publish on its website a notice fully explaining its reasons for doing so.

sections.source_quote: Member States shall provide that competent authorities can apply their powers to impose sanctions under this Directive for the infringements of position limits set in accordance with this Article to: (a) positions held by persons situated or operating in its territory or abroad which exceed the limits on commodity derivative contracts the competent authority has set in relation to contracts on trading venues situated or operating in its territory or economically equivalent OTC contracts; (b) positions held by persons situated or operating in its territory which exceed the limits on commodity derivative contracts set by competent authorities in other Member States.

sections.title: Definitions for investment firm and related terms in financial services
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used throughout the Directive, including 'investment firm', 'investment services and activities', 'client', 'professional client', 'retail client', and other financial terms. It specifies that Member States may include non-legal persons as investment firms if they meet certain protection and supervision criteria. The definitions also cover specific financial instruments, trading venues, and market structures relevant to investment activities. This section lists specific financial regulations that are defined for the purpose of the GDPR. It identifies three directives related to UCITS, market transparency, and financial statements that are relevant to the context of the GDPR. These definitions are provided to clarify the scope of certain financial instruments and entities under the GDPR framework.

requirements.text: Member States may include in the definition of investment firms undertakings which are not legal persons, provided that: (a) their legal status ensures a level of protection for third parties' interests equivalent to that afforded by legal persons; and (b) they are subject to equivalent prudential supervision appropriate to their legal form.
Definitions of financial regulations relevant to the GDPR

sections.title: Commission report on energy derivatives clearing impact
sections.summary: The Commission must prepare a report by 1 January 2018 after consulting ESMA and ACER to assess the potential impact on energy prices and market functioning, feasibility, and benefits of subjecting C6 energy derivative contracts to clearing obligations, risk mitigation techniques, and clearing thresholds. The Commission may extend the 42-month period for delegated acts by two years once and one year further.

sections.title: Professional client waiver procedure requirements
sections.summary: Investment firms must follow a written procedure to allow clients to waive detailed rules of conduct and be treated as professional clients. This requires clients to state in writing their intent to be treated as professional clients, receive a written warning about lost protections and compensation rights, and confirm awareness of consequences in a separate document. Firms must verify client eligibility before accepting waivers and implement written policies to categorize clients. If clients no longer meet professional criteria, firms must take action.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes specific procedural requirements for clients to be treated as professional clients, which impacts cross-border data transfers involving investment firms and clients by defining when and how client categorization occurs.

requirements.text: Clients must state in writing to the investment firm their wish to be treated as a professional client
Investment firms must provide written warning of protections and investor compensation rights lost
Clients must state in writing, in a separate document from the contract, awareness of consequences of losing protections
Investment firms must take reasonable steps to ensure client meets professional requirements before accepting waiver requests
Firms must implement written internal policies to categorize clients
Professional clients must inform investment firms of any changes affecting their categorization
Investment firms must take action if clients no longer fulfill initial professional conditions

sections.source_quote: II.2. Procedure Those clients may waive the benefit of the detailed rules of conduct only where the following procedure is followed: — they must state in writing to the investment firm that they wish to be treated as a professional client, either generally or in respect of a particular investment service or transaction, or type of transaction or product, — the investment firm must give them a clear written warning of the protections and investor compensation rights they may lose, — they must state in writing, in a separate document from the contract, that they are aware of the consequences of losing such protections.
